"Test code R script"  (MM)

The attached R test script shows **no** difference for me (on Fedora 42 Linux)
with a CEST locale (in the shell),
e.g., here

```
f=datetime3_lt_subsec.Rout_Rdev_MM
R-devel --vanilla -s < ..../datetime3_lt_subsec.R > $f 2>&1

f=datetime3_lt_subsec.Rout_R453
R-4.5.3 --vanilla -s < ..../datetime3_lt_subsec.R > $f 2>&1
```

with `R CMD Rdiff` only showing these timing differences

```
   MM@v-lynne:tests$ R CMD Rdiff  datetime3_lt_subsec.Rout_R453
datetime3_lt_subsec.Rout_Rdev_MM
   234,235c234,235
   < last      0.123    0.063   0.209      0.000     0.000
   < total     0.298    0.184   1.690      0.004     0.009

<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18989>

--- Comment #15 from Suharto Anggono (<mailto:suharto_anggono@yahoo.com|suharto_anggono@yahoo.com>) ---
(In reply to Martin Maechler from comment #13)
`x` has `tzone` attribute of length 1. For `thtz` that is a known time zone and
not `"UTC"`, `lcx` has `tzone` attribute of length 3.

Try

    x25 <- x; x. <- x <- lcx

For `thtz` that is a known time zone and not `"UTC"`, after the subassignment

    x[1:25] <- x25[1:25]

, `x[25]$sec == 60` will be `FALSE` in current R devel.

text:
*[Bug 18930] Inconsistent behaviour of merge() on empty DF*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18930>

Gabriel Becker (<mailto:gabembecker@gmail.com|gabembecker@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |gabembecker@gmail.com

--- Comment #1 from Gabriel Becker (<mailto:gabembecker@gmail.com|gabembecker@gmail.com>) ---
Created attachment 3667
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3667&action=edit>
patch to fix merge zero rows x 1 column case

Wow, this one has been in there for 18 years according to blame...

This one ends up being an easy fix. The culprit is that R attempts to grab zero
row data.frames for each of the inputs via `x[FALSE, ]` and `y[FALSE, ]` to
construct the cartesian product for the zero rows case, but when x or y has
only one column, that doesn't grabs the column vector due to the default of
`drop = TRUE`.

Adding `drop = FALSE` fixes both of your cases (neither of which was correct,
though the second was closer).

Patch with the one-line change and regression tests for the two above cases
attached.

Created attachment 3668
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3668&action=edit>
Patch for saveload.c preventing negative or INT_MAX values

## Summary

Two parallel bugs exist in `src/main/saveload.c`, one in each of the two
string-reading functions for the legacy R save format (version 1):

- **MEM-002** — `InStringAscii()`: reads a string byte count via
  `fscanf("%d", &nbytes)` with no bounds check. A crafted ASCII-format
  `.RData` file with `nbytes = -1` causes a NULL pointer write (SIGSEGV).
  `nbytes = INT_MAX` causes a signed integer overflow followed by a massive
  buffer overread.

- **MEM-033** — `InStringBinary()`: reads a string byte count via binary
  `fread` with no bounds check. A crafted binary-format `.RData` file with
  `nbytes = -1` bypasses allocation, leaving `buf == NULL`; the subsequent
  `fread(NULL, 1, (size_t)-1, fp)` is undefined behaviour and crashes
  immediately.

Both functions share the same `static char *buf = NULL` pattern and the same
missing guard. The fix and patch are identical in structure.

---

## Affected Component

- **File:** `src/main/saveload.c`
- **Functions:** `InStringAscii()` (lines 1442–1457), `InStringBinary()` (line
~1584–1599)
- **Mechanism:** Signed `nbytes` read from untrusted file data; negative value
  bypasses the `nbytes >= buflen` allocation guard, leaving `buf == NULL` for
  the subsequent write; `INT_MAX` causes `nbytes + 1` to overflow to 0.

---

## Root Cause

Both functions use the same pattern:

```c
static char *buf = NULL;
static int buflen = 0;

/* InStringAscii: */
int nbytes;
fscanf(fp, "%d", &nbytes);          /* attacker-controlled; can be -1 */

/* InStringBinary: */
int nbytes = InIntegerBinary(fp, unused);   /* attacker-controlled; can be -1
*/

/* Shared flaw in both: */
if (nbytes >= buflen) {             /* -1 >= 0 is FALSE — allocation skipped */
    if (buf) newbuf = realloc(buf, nbytes + 1);
    else     newbuf = malloc(nbytes + 1);   /* nbytes+1 overflows if INT_MAX */
    buf = newbuf;
}
/* buf is still NULL on first call with nbytes < 0 */
buf[i] = '\0';                      /* MEM-002: NULL write → SIGSEGV */
fread(buf, 1, nbytes, fp);          /* MEM-033: fread(NULL,...) → UB/crash */
```

---

## Reproducible Examples

### MEM-002 — ASCII format (nbytes = -1)

```r
# Step 1: Create a valid version-1 ASCII save file
x <- "hello"
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".RData")
suppressWarnings(save(x, file = f, ascii = TRUE, version = 1))

# Step 2: Patch the string byte count from 5 to -1
# The file contains a line " 5 hello"; replace it with "-1"
content <- readLines(f, warn = FALSE)
idx <- which(grepl("^ 5 hello$", content))
content[idx] <- "-1"
writeLines(content, f)

# Step 3: Load — triggers InStringAscii() with nbytes = -1
# Before fix: SIGSEGV (NULL write at address 0x0)
# After fix:  Error: negative or overflowing string size in ascii read
tryCatch(
    load(f),
    error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
)
unlink(f)
```

### MEM-033 — Binary format (nbytes = -1)

```r
# Step 1: Create a valid old-format binary save file
x <- "hello"
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".RData")
suppressWarnings(save(x, file = f, compress = FALSE, version = 1))

# Step 2: Locate and patch the big-endian int32 string length (0x00000005)
# to 0xFFFFFFFF (-1 signed).

Patch for saveload.c preventing negative or INT_MAX values

## Summary

Two parallel bugs exist in

string-reading functions for the legacy R save format (version 1):

- **MEM-002** —

with no bounds check. A crafted ASCII-format

causes a signed integer overflow followed by a massive
  buffer overread.

- **MEM-033** —
: reads a string byte count via binary
with no bounds check. A crafted binary-format

is undefined behaviour and crashes

Both functions share the same

pattern and the same
missing guard. The fix and patch are identical in structure.

---

## Affected Component

- **File:**

read from untrusted file data; negative value

to overflow to 0.

---

## Root Cause

Both functions use the same pattern:

r
# Step 1: Create a valid version-1 ASCII save file
x <- "hello"
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".RData")
suppressWarnings(save(x, file = f, ascii = TRUE, version = 1))

# Step 2: Patch the string byte count from 5 to -1
# The file contains a line " 5 hello"; replace it with "-1"
content <- readLines(f, warn = FALSE)
idx <- which(grepl("^ 5 hello$", content))
content[idx] <- "-1"
writeLines(content, f)

# Step 3: Load — triggers InStringAscii() with nbytes = -1
# Before fix: SIGSEGV (NULL write at address 0x0)
# After fix:  Error: negative or overflowing string size in ascii read
tryCatch(
    load(f),
    error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
)
unlink(f)

### MEM-033 — Binary format (nbytes = -1)

```r
# Step 1: Create a valid old-format binary save file
x <- "hello"
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".RData")
suppressWarnings(save(x, file = f, compress = FALSE, version = 1))

# Step 2: Locate and patch the big-endian int32 string length (0x00000005)
# to 0xFFFFFFFF (-1 signed).

text:
raw_data <- readBin(f, what = "raw", n = <http://file.info|file.info>(f)$size)
# Find the pattern 00 00 00 05 followed by the ASCII bytes of "hello"
target <- as.raw(c(0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x05, chartr("", "", "hello")))
pos <- which(apply(embed(as.integer(raw_data), length(target))[,
rev(seq_len(length(target)))],
                   1, function(r) all(r == as.integer(target))))
if (length(pos) == 1L) {
    raw_data[pos + 0:3] <- as.raw(c(0xff, 0xff, 0xff, 0xff))
    writeBin(raw_data, f)
}

# Step 3: Load — triggers InStringBinary() with nbytes = -1
# Before fix: fread(NULL, 1, SIZE_MAX, fp) → UB / SIGSEGV
# After fix:  Error: negative or overflowing string size in binary read
tryCatch(
    load(f),
    error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
)
unlink(f)
```

---

## Observed Behaviour (Before Fix)

**MEM-002 (ASCII):** R crashes with `SIGSEGV` (NULL pointer write to address
0x0). Under ASAN: `ERROR: AddressSanitizer: SEGV on unknown address
0x000000000000`.

**MEM-033 (Binary):** `fread(NULL, 1, (size_t)-1, fp)` — undefined behaviour;
typically a `SIGSEGV` or assertion failure in the C library's `fread`.

**After fix (both):** Clean R-level error:
```
Error: negative or overflowing string size in ascii read   # MEM-002
Error: negative or overflowing string size in binary read  # MEM-033
```

Error: negative or overflowing string size in binary read  # MEM-033

Created attachment 3669
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3669&action=edit>
Fix serialize.c to prevent the overflows

## Summary

Three related vulnerabilities in `src/main/serialize.c` are all triggered by
loading a crafted `.rds` file via `readRDS()` or `unserialize()`:

| ID      | Location                        | Class                        |
Score |
|---------|---------------------------------|------------------------------|-------|
| MEM-005 | `ReadItem_Recursive()`, VECSXP loop | C stack overflow (no depth
limit) | 15 |
| MEM-006 | `ReadItem()`, VECSXP/STRSXP length | Negative/huge length →
`allocVector()` UB | 9 |
| MEM-007 | `ReadItem()`, BCREPDEF `nreps`  | Negative `nreps` →
`allocVector()` UB | 9 |

All three are reachable from any call site that deserializes untrusted data:
`readRDS()`, `load()`, `unserialize()`, and package `.rds` data files.

---

## Affected Component

- **File:** `src/main/serialize.c`
- **Functions:** `ReadItem()`, `ReadItem_Recursive()`
- **Patch:** `review/patches/patch_MEM005_MEM006_MEM007.patch`

---

## MEM-005 — C Stack Overflow via Deeply Nested List

### Description

`ReadItem_Recursive()` calls `ReadItem()` recursively for each VECSXP
element. The existing `R_ReadItemDepth` counter is incremented around
recursive reads but is never compared against a limit, and `R_CheckStack()`
is absent from the VECSXP element loop. A crafted `.rds` file with 50,000
levels of list nesting overflows the C stack, terminating R with `segfault
from C stack overflow`.

### Key Design Facts

`REFSXP` (back-references) do **not** cause recursion — `GetReadRef` is a
constant-time table lookup. The recursion comes purely from structural depth:
each `list(list(...))` nesting level adds one C stack frame. `R_CheckStack()`
is present in `ReadItem_Iterative` (LISTSXP/pairlist CDR chains) but absent
from the VECSXP loop.

```c
case VECSXP:
    len = ReadLENGTH(stream);
    PROTECT(s = allocVector(type, len));
    R_ReadItemDepth++;
    for (count = 0; count < len; ++count) {
        /* R_CheckStack() absent here */
        SET_VECTOR_ELT(s, count, ReadItem(ref_table, stream));  /* recurses */
    }
    R_ReadItemDepth--;
```

### Reproducible Example

```r
# Craft a 50,000-deep nested list RDS in ASCII format (no C recursion required)
depth <- 50000L
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")
con <- file(f, "w")
# ASCII RDS header (format version 3, R 4.7.0 written, R 3.5.0 minimum)
cat("A\n3\n263936\n197888\n5\nUTF-8\n", file = con)
# depth VECSXP wrappers of length 1
for (i in seq_len(depth))
    cat("19\n1\n", file = con)
# leaf: CHARSXP "leaf", UTF-8, 4 bytes
cat("262153\n4\nleaf\n", file = con)
close(con)

# File size: ~250 KB; 100,009 lines
# Before fix: segfault from C stack overflow — R process terminates
# After fix:  Error in readRDS(f) : unserialization depth limit exceeded
tryCatch(readRDS(f), error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e),
"\n"))
```

### Fix Applied

Add a depth limit check in `ReadItem()` at entry:

```c
/* Option A: hard limit at ReadItem() entry */
static SEXP ReadItem(SEXP ref_table, R_inpstream_t stream) {
    if (R_ReadItemDepth > R_MAX_READ_DEPTH)   /* e.g. R_MAX_READ_DEPTH = 500000
*/
        error(_("unserialization depth limit exceeded"));
    ...
}

/* Option B: R_CheckStack() in the VECSXP loop (lighter-weight) */
case VECSXP:
    ...

## Summary

Three related vulnerabilities in

limit) | 15 |
| MEM-006 |

UB | 9 |

All three are reachable from any call site that deserializes untrusted data:

---

## MEM-005 — C Stack Overflow via Deeply Nested List

### Description

recursive reads but is never compared against a limit, and
is absent from the VECSXP element loop. A crafted

file with 50,000
levels of list nesting overflows the C stack, terminating R with `segfault
from C stack overflow`.

### Key Design Facts

constant-time table lookup. The recursion comes purely from structural depth:
each
nesting level adds one C stack frame.

(LISTSXP/pairlist CDR chains) but absent
from the VECSXP loop.

r
# Craft a 50,000-deep nested list RDS in ASCII format (no C recursion required)
depth <- 50000L
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")
con <- file(f, "w")
# ASCII RDS header (format version 3, R 4.7.0 written, R 3.5.0 minimum)
cat("A\n3\n263936\n197888\n5\nUTF-8\n", file = con)
# depth VECSXP wrappers of length 1
for (i in seq_len(depth))
    cat("19\n1\n", file = con)
# leaf: CHARSXP "leaf", UTF-8, 4 bytes
cat("262153\n4\nleaf\n", file = con)
close(con)

# File size: ~250 KB; 100,009 lines
# Before fix: segfault from C stack overflow — R process terminates
# After fix:  Error in readRDS(f) : unserialization depth limit exceeded
tryCatch(readRDS(f), error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e),
"\n"))

### Fix Applied

Add a depth limit check in

text:
for (count = 0; count < len; ++count) {
        R_CheckStack();
        SET_VECTOR_ELT(s, count, ReadItem(ref_table, stream));
    }
```

Option A (explicit limit) is more predictable and portable across platforms
with different default stack sizes.

### Attack Surface

Any R service that processes user-supplied `.rds` files is a reliable
denial-of-service target: `readRDS()` on an uploaded file (Shiny, REST API),
`unserialize()` on attacker-controlled binary data, package `.rds` data
files, or shared `.RData` workspace files.

---

## MEM-006 — Negative/Oversized Vector Length in ReadItem()

### Description

`ReadItem()` reads a length field from the stream via `InInteger(stream)` and
passes it directly to `allocVector()` without bounds checking. A crafted
`.rds` file supplying a negative length (e.g., `-1` = `0xffffffff` in XDR
big-endian) triggers undefined behaviour in `allocVector()`, which on 64-bit
systems interprets the sign-extended value as an enormous `R_xlen_t`,
producing a multi-gigabyte allocation attempt or an immediate crash.

### Root Cause

```c
/* Before fix: */
length = InInteger(stream);
/* length used directly — could be negative or >= MAXELTSIZE */
allocVector(type, length);
```

### Reproducible Example

```r
# Craft a minimal XDR RDS file with VECSXP length = -1
bytes <- as.raw(c(
  0x58, 0x0a,              # 'X', '\n'  — XDR format marker
  0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x02,  # format version 2
  0x00, 0x03, 0x05, 0x00,  # writer R version (3.5.0)
  0x00, 0x02, 0x03, 0x00,  # minimum R version (2.3.0)
  0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x13,  # VECSXP (type 19 = 0x13), no flags
  0xff, 0xff, 0xff, 0xff   # length = -1 (big-endian int32)
))
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")
writeBin(bytes, f)

# Before fix: UB in allocVector() — crash or ~4 GB allocation attempt
# After fix:  Error in readRDS(f) : invalid length in ReadItem
tryCatch(readRDS(f), error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e),
"\n"))
```

Under ASAN (`build-asan`), the unpatched version produces an
`allocation-size-too-big` report before any crash.

### Fix Applied

```c
/* After: */
if (length < 0 || length >= MAXELTSIZE)
    error(_("invalid length in ReadItem"));
```

---

## MEM-007 — Negative nreps in Bytecode Constant Pool Deserialization

### Description

`ReadItem()` reads the `nreps` field for bytecode objects (the `BCREPDEF`
case) via `InInteger(stream)` and passes it directly to
`allocVector(VECSXP, nreps)`. A crafted `.rds` containing a bytecode object
with `nreps = -1` triggers the same `allocVector()` undefined behaviour as
MEM-006.

### Root Cause

```c
/* Before fix: */
PROTECT(reps = allocVector(VECSXP, InInteger(stream)));
/* InInteger() could return any int, including negative */
```

### Reproducible Example

```r
# Craft a minimal XDR RDS file: BCODESXP with BCREPDEF and nreps = -1
header <- as.raw(c(
  0x58, 0x0a,              # 'X', '\n'  — XDR format marker
  0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x02,  # format version 2
  0x00, 0x03, 0x05, 0x00,  # writer R version (3.5.0)
  0x00, 0x02, 0x03, 0x00   # minimum R version (2.3.0)
))
object <- as.raw(c(
  0x00, 0x00, 0x02, 0x15,  # BCODESXP (type 21) | BCREPDEF flag (0x200)
  0xff, 0xff, 0xff, 0xff   # nreps = -1
))
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")
writeBin(c(header, object), f)

# Before fix: allocVector(VECSXP, -1) — UB, crash, or enormous allocation
# After fix:  Error in readRDS(f) : invalid bytecode object
tryCatch(readRDS(f), error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e),
"\n"))
```

Under ASAN (`build-asan`), the unpatched version raises
`allocation-size-too-big` immediately after `nreps` is read.

### Fix Applied

```c
/* After: */
int nreps = InInteger(stream);
if (nreps < 0)
    error(_("invalid bytecode object"));
PROTECT(reps = allocVector(VECSXP, nreps));
```

elements.text:
Option A (explicit limit) is more predictable and portable across platforms
with different default stack sizes.

### Attack Surface

Any R service that processes user-supplied `.rds` files is a reliable
denial-of-service target: `readRDS()` on an uploaded file (Shiny, REST API),
`unserialize()` on attacker-controlled binary data, package `.rds` data
files, or shared `.RData` workspace files.

---

## MEM-006 — Negative/Oversized Vector Length in ReadItem()

### Description

`ReadItem()` reads a length field from the stream via `InInteger(stream)` and
passes it directly to `allocVector()` without bounds checking. A crafted
`.rds` file supplying a negative length (e.g., `-1` = `0xffffffff` in XDR
big-endian) triggers undefined behaviour in `allocVector()`, which on 64-bit
systems interprets the sign-extended value as an enormous `R_xlen_t`,
producing a multi-gigabyte allocation attempt or an immediate crash.

### Root Cause

```c
/* Before fix: */
length = InInteger(stream);
/* length used directly — could be negative or >= MAXELTSIZE */
allocVector(type, length);

r
# Craft a minimal XDR RDS file with VECSXP length = -1
bytes <- as.raw(c(
  0x58, 0x0a,              # 'X', '\n'  — XDR format marker
  0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x02,  # format version 2
  0x00, 0x03, 0x05, 0x00,  # writer R version (3.5.0)
  0x00, 0x02, 0x03, 0x00,  # minimum R version (2.3.0)
  0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x13,  # VECSXP (type 19 = 0x13), no flags
  0xff, 0xff, 0xff, 0xff   # length = -1 (big-endian int32)
))
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")
writeBin(bytes, f)

# Before fix: UB in allocVector() — crash or ~4 GB allocation attempt
# After fix:  Error in readRDS(f) : invalid length in ReadItem
tryCatch(readRDS(f), error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e),
"\n"))

report before any crash.

### Fix Applied

---

### Description

header <- as.raw(c(

  0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x02,  # format version 2
  0x00, 0x03, 0x05, 0x00,  # writer R version (3.5.0)
  0x00, 0x02, 0x03, 0x00   # minimum R version (2.3.0)
))
object <- as.raw(c(
  0x00, 0x00, 0x02, 0x15,  # BCODESXP (type 21) | BCREPDEF flag (0x200)
  0xff, 0xff, 0xff, 0xff   # nreps = -1
))
f <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")
writeBin(c(header, object), f)

# Before fix: allocVector(VECSXP, -1) — UB, crash, or enormous allocation
# After fix:  Error in readRDS(f) : invalid bytecode object
tryCatch(readRDS(f), error = function(e) cat("Caught:", conditionMessage(e),

*[Bug 19048] New: textConnection can cause crash with large strings*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19048>

            Bug ID: 19048
           Summary: textConnection can cause crash with large strings
           Product: R
           Version: R-devel (trunk)
          Hardware: All
                OS: All
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: major
          Priority: P5
         Component: I/O
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:bill.r-project@denney.ws|bill.r-project@denney.ws>

Created attachment 3670
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3670&action=edit>
Fix for scan.c

## Summary

`scan()` in `src/main/scan.c` maintains a dynamic read buffer `buf` whose size
is tracked in `int nbuf`. When growing the buffer, `nbuf` is repeatedly doubled
(`nbuf *= 2`) without an overflow check. Once `nbuf` exceeds `INT_MAX/2`, the
next doubling overflows signed `int`, producing a negative size that is then
passed to `R_Realloc()`. This results in a very large allocation (UB sign
extension on most platforms) and a potential heap overflow when the buffer is
subsequently filled.

Five code paths in `scan.c` grow `nbuf` independently; all five were
unguarded.

---

## Affected Component

- **File:** `src/main/scan.c`
- **Function:** multiple token-accumulation loops
- **Lines:** buffer growth paths in `scanchar()` and related functions
- **Mechanism:** `nbuf *= 2` wraps to negative when `nbuf > INT_MAX/2`.

---

## Root Cause

```c
/* Before — repeated in 5 places: */
nbuf *= 2;
buf = R_Realloc(buf, nbuf, char);
/* nbuf could be negative after overflow */
```

---

## Reproducible Example

The integer overflow occurs when a single token read by `scan()` exceeds
`INT_MAX / 2` bytes (~1 GB). Constructing such input in memory is practical on
64-bit systems with sufficient RAM. The `textConnection()` approach avoids
requiring a large temporary file.

```r
# Trigger the scan() buffer overflow guard (or overflow UB on unpatched R).
# Warning: on unpatched R this may crash the session or allocate ~2-4 GB.

# Build a single "token" of ~1.1 GB (just over INT_MAX/2 bytes) using a
# textConnection, then call scan() on it.
# Use nchars as an alias for the token length.
token_len <- .Machine$integer.max %/% 2L + 100L  # just over INT_MAX/2

# textConnection feeds character data to scan(); construct a string of that
# length using strrep (available since R 3.3.0).
big_token <- strrep("a", token_len)

con <- textConnection(big_token)

# Before fix: nbuf overflows to negative → R_Realloc(buf, <negative>, char)
#             → allocates enormous block → crash / heap corruption
# After fix:  Error in scan() : string too long in scan()
tryCatch(
  scan(con, what = character(), quiet = TRUE),
  error = function(e) message("Caught: ", conditionMessage(e))
)
close(con)
```

Expected output after the patch:
```
Caught: string too long in scan()
```

**Note on memory requirements:** `strrep("a", ~1.07 GB)` allocates ~1 GB of
character data. On machines with less than ~3 GB of available memory, the
`strrep()` call itself may fail before reaching `scan()`. In that case the
bug can be triggered equivalently via a crafted 1 GB text file:

```r
# Alternative: write a 1 GB single-token file then scan it
f <- tempfile()
writeBin(chartr("", "", strrep("a", 1073741824L)), f)
# scan() the raw file
con <- file(f, "rb")
tryCatch(
  scan(con, what = character(), quiet = TRUE),
  error = function(e) message("Caught: ", conditionMessage(e))
)
close(con)
unlink(f)
```

---

## Fix Applied

Added an overflow guard before each doubling:

```c
/* After — each of 5 sites: */
if (nbuf > INT_MAX / 2) error(_("string too long in scan()"));
nbuf *= 2;
buf = R_Realloc(buf, nbuf, char);
```

**Fix location:** `src/main/scan.c`, five buffer growth sites.
**Patch:** `review/patches/patch_MEM010.patch`

---

## Severity

text:
Score 9 (L=3, I=3). Triggered by `scan()` on an input with a token longer
than ~1 GB — achievable on 64-bit systems with a crafted text file or
`textConnection`. The resulting heap overflow from the mis-sized realloc is
a reliable crash and potential memory corruption.
